source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
policy on its partners.109 The institutions and private actors connected to the
CSCS could create a new data and corporate governance hub. ‘[C]ontrol over
the massive amounts of data produced in China’s cyberspace may well become
high-value currency in the political stakes’.110 Just as the SCS makes surveil-
lance subtler than it was before,111 the characteristics of the CSCS make the
political usage of a technological hub subtler. The ‘sites’ for regulating this
data and deﬁning the contours and usage of the SCS, eg, institutions, minis-
tries, agencies and private actors, are crucial for transnational law as a political
project: ‘They are signiﬁcant in generating perceptions of the legitimacy of the
disciplines that these institutions impose upon states as common sense.’112 Law
is not just a solution to a problem but the law is a tool to shape interpretation. It
‘produces and stocks interpretive patterns and visions of life which shape
people’s ways of organizing social experience, giving it meaning, qualifying it
as normal and just or deviant and unjust’.113 The ‘sites’ that deﬁne and regulate
and use the CSCS are located in China and thus have an inﬂuence on the per-
ception of the legitimacy of the CSCS and its values. They feed into the trans-
national conception of fairness in global trade and cooperation as well as the
requirements for cooperations, introducing a certain (political) conception of
trustworthiness as ‘normal’ and ‘just’.
    It can be assumed that in the long run, the concept of ‘trustworthiness’ as
understood and used in the CSCS will evoke secondary eﬀects on corpor-
ations, institutions and individuals that are not present in China. This has
already become a reality with regard to NGOs114 as well as European poli-
ticians who intend to visit Taiwan and face hindrances not just by Chinese
oﬃcials but by their own country’s government that fears economic conse-
quences.115 This indicates that future practices within the CSCS might also
strengthen a system that is built on buying loyalty of inﬂuential individuals
for political and strategic aims.116 The CSCS can serve as a tool to enhance
the inﬂuence of the CCP by co-optation,117 binding foreign individuals such
